news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in biological understanding, innovative communication methods challenging time constraints, and enhanced quality control in scientific research dissemination.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la compréhension de la biologie, des méthodes de communication innovantes qui repoussent les limites du temps, ainsi qu'un contrôle qualité renforcé dans la diffusion des résultats de la recherche scientifique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances en el conocimiento de la biología, métodos de comunicación innovadores que desafían las limitaciones de tiempo y un mayor control de calidad en la difusión de la investigación científica.

articles.title: All life runs on 20 amino acids. These cells run key machinery on just 19
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01396-wAI-guided redesign of protein alphabet in bacteria could unlock new ways to build synthetic organisms.
articles.content:
Scientists equipped Escherichia coli (example pictured) with ribosomes that lacked the amino acid isoleucine.Credit: James Cavallini/Science Photo Library

All life on Earth depends on the same molecular alphabet: 20 amino acids that cells string together to make proteins. But now, scientists have reengineered bacteria to run a core part of their cellular machinery with just 19 of those amino acids — a feat akin to rewriting one act of a Shakespearean play without a common letter like ‘R’ while keeping the text intelligible. The work is reported today in Science1.

“It’s very exciting that it’s possible,” says Julius Fredens, a synthetic biologist at the National University of Singapore who was not involved in the research.

The work offers a blueprint for engineering cells with capabilities beyond those found in nature, the authors say, while also hinting at a simpler past when early life relied on a more limited set of building blocks.

Shrinking the script

Researchers have long sought to rewrite the genetic code of life, both to expand what cells can do and to probe the basic rules of life. For example, scientists have streamlined DNA by removing sequences that encode the same amino acid as other stretches. But most researchers have left the ‘canonical’ 20 amino acids untouched, because even small changes to a protein’s amino-acid sequence tend to disrupt its function.

AI can write genomes — how long until it creates synthetic life?

The challenge of subtracting a letter from the vocabulary of proteins intrigued Harris Wang — though his early attempts fell short. A synthetic biologist at Columbia University in New York City, Wang initially tried simply swapping one amino acid — isoleucine — with others that differ slightly in size and shape, but fewer than half of his modified proteins remaining functional.

Wang shelved the project for a few years, until a new generation of artificial-intelligence tools began to change what was possible. Systems such as AlphaFold can predict a protein’s 3D structure, and various protein language models can now suggest entirely new amino-acid sequences that fold and function. Crucially for Wang, these tools could point to non-intuitive ways that might allow replacement of isoleucine without undermining protein performance.

Still, reworking all 4,000-plus proteins in the bacterium Escherichia coli seemed too daunting a challenge. Instead, Wang chose a more focused, if still ambitious, target: the ribosome.

Proving the principle

The ribosome — a complex of more than 50 proteins and catalytic RNA — sits at the heart of the cell, translating genetic instructions into proteins. If such a critical system could operate without isoleucine, Wang reasoned, the same approach might extend to the rest of the proteome.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers successfully engineered bacteria to operate essential cellular machinery using only 19 amino acids instead of the canonical 20. This achievement demonstrates the power of AI-guided protein design and synthetic biology, potentially enabling new ways to build synthetic organisms and expanding our understanding of life's molecular basis. The impact is broad, affecting biotechnology, synthetic biology, and fundamental biology, and the article provides sufficient detail about the methods and implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create bacteria that work with just 19 amino acids
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – All living things on Earth use 20 amino acids to build proteins, which are essential for life. These amino acids are like letters in a language that cells use to make the tools and machines they need to survive.

**What happened** – Researchers at Columbia University changed bacteria called Escherichia coli so that a key part of their cell machinery, the ribosome, works using only 19 amino acids instead of 20. They removed one amino acid, isoleucine, and used artificial intelligence to redesign the proteins so they still functioned properly.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have shown that such an important cellular system can work without one of the usual amino acids. It suggests cells can be simplified and engineered in new ways, which could help create synthetic organisms with new abilities or help us understand how early life might have worked with fewer building blocks.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to apply this AI-guided method to redesign other proteins in the bacteria and eventually build synthetic life forms with altered protein codes. This could open the door to new kinds of biotechnology and medicine.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists used AI to redesign bacteria so their core machinery runs with only 19 amino acids, showing new possibilities for engineering life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des bactéries fonctionnant avec seulement 19 acides aminés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Tous les êtres vivants utilisent 20 acides aminés pour fabriquer des protéines, qui sont essentielles à la vie. Ces acides aminés sont comme les lettres d'une langue que les cellules utilisent pour créer leurs outils et machines nécessaires à leur survie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Columbia ont modifié des bactéries appelées Escherichia coli pour que leur machinerie cellulaire principale, le ribosome, fonctionne avec seulement 19 acides aminés au lieu de 20. Ils ont supprimé un acide aminé, l’isoleucine, et ont utilisé l’intelligence artificielle pour repenser les protéines afin qu’elles fonctionnent toujours correctement.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des scientifiques montrent qu’un système cellulaire aussi important peut fonctionner sans un des acides aminés habituels. Cela suggère que les cellules peuvent être simplifiées et modifiées de nouvelles façons, ce qui pourrait aider à créer des organismes synthétiques avec de nouvelles capacités ou à mieux comprendre comment la vie primitive fonctionnait avec moins de composants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent appliquer cette méthode guidée par l’IA pour repenser d’autres protéines dans la bactérie et, à terme, construire des formes de vie synthétiques avec des codes protéiques modifiés. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouvelles biotechnologies et traitements médicaux.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé l’IA pour repenser des bactéries dont la machinerie centrale fonctionne avec seulement 19 acides aminés, montrant de nouvelles possibilités pour l’ingénierie du vivant.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean bacterias que funcionan con solo 19 aminoácidos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Todos los seres vivos usan 20 aminoácidos para construir proteínas, que son esenciales para la vida. Estos aminoácidos son como letras en un idioma que las células usan para fabricar sus herramientas y máquinas necesarias para sobrevivir.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Columbia modificaron bacterias llamadas Escherichia coli para que una parte clave de su maquinaria celular, el ribosoma, funcione usando solo 19 aminoácidos en lugar de 20. Eliminaron un aminoácido, la isoleucina, y usaron inteligencia artificial para rediseñar las proteínas y que siguieran funcionando bien.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos demuestran que un sistema celular tan importante puede funcionar sin uno de los aminoácidos habituales. Esto sugiere que las células pueden simplificarse y diseñarse de nuevas formas, lo que podría ayudar a crear organismos sintéticos con nuevas habilidades o a entender mejor cómo la vida temprana funcionaba con menos componentes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan usar este método guiado por IA para rediseñar otras proteínas en la bacteria y, eventualmente, construir formas de vida sintéticas con códigos de proteínas alterados. Esto podría abrir nuevas posibilidades en biotecnología y medicina.

**Frase clave** – Científicos usaron IA para rediseñar bacterias cuyo núcleo celular funciona con solo 19 aminoácidos, mostrando nuevas posibilidades para la ingeniería de la vida.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized Escherichia coli bacterium composed of interlocking puzzle pieces, with one puzzle piece shaped like the amino acid isoleucine gently removed and floating nearby, while a glowing ribosome inside the bacterium continues to function smoothly, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and subtle highlights.

articles.title: We have figured out a new way to send messages into the past
articles.summary: A technique inspired by the film Interstellar suggests a new way of communicating backwards in time, but it could help improve conventional communication systems as well
articles.content:
A technique inspired by the film Interstellar suggests a new way of communicating backwards in time, but it could help improve conventional communication systems as well

Closed time-like curves offer a route to the past Roman Budnikov/Alamy

What if you could send a message into the past? The laws of physics don’t forbid it – and in fact, in some cases, communicating backwards in time might actually be easier than the usual direction.

The possibility of sending a message to the past emerges from a particular kind of solution to the equations of general relativity, which is our best theory of how space-time, the fabric of reality, behaves. Every object in the universe follows a path through space-time, and one such path that is allowed by general relativity is called a closed time-like curve (CTC), which sees an object travel into the future before returning to the past and ending up in the present, forming a time loop.

There is just one catch: at cosmic scales, building a CTC would mean bending space-time until it closes in on itself, which would require an impossibly large amount of energy. That seems to rule out sending a message back in time – but quantum entanglement might offer a solution.

When two particles are entangled, the state of one is always sensitive to the state of the other, even when they are extremely distant. Instead of interpreting this as the two being part of some very spread-out quantum state, some physicists posit that one particle’s sensitivity to what is happening to the other comes from that second particle sending messages backwards in time to the first, alerting it how to react later.

Not everyone agrees with this interpretation, but in 2010, Seth Lloyd at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and his colleagues used entangled particles of light, or photons, to mimic a quantum CTC. “It was the equivalent of sending a photon a few nanoseconds backwards in time, and having it try to kill its former self,” says Lloyd.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Now, Lloyd and his colleagues have imagined a new version of the experiment in which something goes wrong and the CTC becomes crackly and noisy, like a faulty phone line. Evaluating the messaging capacity of a noisy channel is a common problem in information theory, and using this framework, the team found that surprisingly, not only is communication with the past still possible, but it actually works better than an equivalently noisy phone line operating in the conventional direction of time.

Team member Kaiyuan Ji, also at MIT, says the team was inspired by the climax of Interstellar. At the end of the film (spoiler alert), an astronaut played by Matthew McConaughey sends a message to his daughter in the past by manipulating the hands on her watch, using something that appears to operate like a CTC. The researchers treated this as a noisy quantum channel and calculated that, unlike with conventional messaging, a backwards time-travelling message would still be legible, because the sender can use their memories of the past. “The father remembers how the daughter decodes his future message, so he can instruct himself on what is the best way to encode the message,” says Ji.

While sending messages backwards in time isn’t a practical issue, better communication strategies for noisy devices are, says Lloyd. “Nobody’s built an actual physical, closed time-like curve, and there are reasons to think it’s very hard to make one. But all channels are noisy,” he says. In fact, Lloyd says the new result ought to be straightforward to turn into an experiment similar to the 2010 quantum CTC made with photons. This could then let the team investigate real-life noisy channels and possibly uncover new ways of using them even for conventional communication.

Andreas Winter at the University of Cologne in Germany says the new work illuminates how different kinds of feedback, such as a sender in the future using their memory, can enhance communication protocols, but practical applications are rather unlikely. “As far as we know, time travel or signalling back in time is not possible in our world. We don’t know of any mechanism that would make it possible,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific breakthrough in quantum communication inspired by theoretical physics and a popular film, suggesting a new method to improve communication systems by mimicking backward time messaging. This has broad significance for advancing communication technology and understanding quantum information, with potential practical benefits for noisy communication channels.
articles.personality_title: Scientists explore new quantum method to improve communication using ideas from time travel
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered if it’s possible to send messages backward in time. This idea comes from the laws of physics and a concept called closed time-like curves (CTCs), which are paths through space and time that loop back to the past. Although building a real CTC would require an enormous amount of energy, researchers have looked for ways to mimic this idea using quantum physics.

**What happened** – A team at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) used pairs of entangled particles of light, called photons, to create a kind of simulated time loop. They studied what happens when the communication channel becomes noisy, like a bad phone connection. Surprisingly, they found that sending messages backward in time using this method could work better than sending them forward through the same noisy channel. Their work was inspired by the movie Interstellar, where a character sends a message to the past using a time loop.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it suggests new ways to improve communication systems that have to deal with noise and errors. While actual time travel or sending messages to the past isn’t possible with today’s technology, the methods used in this research can help design better ways to send information in the real world. For example, devices like phones and computers often struggle with noisy signals, and this work could lead to clearer messages and stronger connections.

**What’s next step** – The researchers plan to turn their calculations into real experiments using photons, similar to their previous work. These experiments will test if their ideas can improve real communication channels that face noise and interference. If successful, this could help develop new technologies for clearer and more reliable communication.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By using quantum physics to imitate time loops, scientists have found a new way to improve communication through noisy channels, inspired by the idea of sending messages into the past.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques explorent une nouvelle méthode quantique pour améliorer la communication grâce à des idées de voyage dans le temps
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps s’il est possible d’envoyer des messages dans le passé. Cette idée vient des lois de la physique et d’un concept appelé courbes fermées de type temps (CTC), des chemins dans l’espace-temps qui bouclent vers le passé. Bien que construire une vraie CTC demanderait une énergie énorme, les chercheurs ont cherché à imiter cette idée grâce à la physique quantique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe du Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) a utilisé des paires de particules de lumière intriquées, appelées photons, pour créer une sorte de boucle temporelle simulée. Ils ont étudié ce qui se passe quand la communication devient bruyante, comme une mauvaise connexion téléphonique. Étonnamment, ils ont découvert que l’envoi de messages vers le passé avec cette méthode pourrait mieux fonctionner que l’envoi vers le futur dans le même canal bruyant. Leur travail s’est inspiré du film Interstellar, où un personnage envoie un message dans le passé grâce à une boucle temporelle.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle suggère de nouvelles façons d’améliorer les systèmes de communication qui doivent gérer le bruit et les erreurs. Même si le voyage dans le temps ou l’envoi de messages dans le passé n’est pas possible avec la technologie actuelle, les méthodes utilisées dans cette recherche peuvent aider à concevoir de meilleures façons d’envoyer des informations dans le monde réel. Par exemple, les appareils comme les téléphones et ordinateurs ont souvent des problèmes avec des signaux bruyants, et ce travail pourrait conduire à des messages plus clairs et des connexions plus solides.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de transformer leurs calculs en expériences réelles avec des photons, comme dans leurs travaux précédents. Ces expériences testeront si leurs idées peuvent améliorer les canaux de communication réels affectés par le bruit et les interférences. En cas de succès, cela pourrait aider à développer de nouvelles technologies pour une communication plus claire et fiable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En utilisant la physique quantique pour imiter des boucles temporelles, des scientifiques ont trouvé une nouvelle façon d’améliorer la communication dans des canaux bruyants, inspirée par l’idée d’envoyer des messages dans le passé.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos exploran un nuevo método cuántico para mejorar la comunicación usando ideas de viajes en el tiempo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos se han preguntado durante mucho tiempo si es posible enviar mensajes al pasado. Esta idea proviene de las leyes de la física y de un concepto llamado curvas temporales cerradas (CTCs), que son caminos en el espacio-tiempo que regresan al pasado. Aunque construir una CTC real requeriría una enorme cantidad de energía, los investigadores han buscado formas de imitar esta idea usando la física cuántica.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo del Instituto Tecnológico de Massachusetts (MIT) usó pares de partículas de luz entrelazadas, llamadas fotones, para crear una especie de bucle temporal simulado. Estudiaron qué ocurre cuando el canal de comunicación se vuelve ruidoso, como una mala conexión telefónica. Sorprendentemente, descubrieron que enviar mensajes hacia el pasado con este método podría funcionar mejor que enviarlos hacia el futuro a través del mismo canal ruidoso. Su trabajo se inspiró en la película Interstellar, donde un personaje envía un mensaje al pasado usando un bucle temporal.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque sugiere nuevas formas de mejorar los sistemas de comunicación que deben lidiar con ruido y errores. Aunque el viaje en el tiempo o enviar mensajes al pasado no es posible con la tecnología actual, los métodos usados en esta investigación pueden ayudar a diseñar mejores formas de enviar información en el mundo real. Por ejemplo, dispositivos como teléfonos y computadoras a menudo tienen problemas con señales ruidosas, y este trabajo podría llevar a mensajes más claros y conexiones más fuertes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean convertir sus cálculos en experimentos reales usando fotones, similar a su trabajo anterior. Estos experimentos probarán si sus ideas pueden mejorar canales de comunicación reales que enfrentan ruido e interferencias. Si tienen éxito, esto podría ayudar a desarrollar nuevas tecnologías para una comunicación más clara y confiable.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al usar la física cuántica para imitar bucles temporales, los científicos han encontrado una nueva forma de mejorar la comunicación a través de canales ruidosos, inspirados en la idea de enviar mensajes al pasado.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, softly glowing network of intertwined light paths forming a looping spiral that folds back onto itself like a closed time-like curve, with delicate, translucent photons traveling along these loops, set against a warm, muted background of gentle earth tones and soft blues, symbolizing quantum entanglement and messages sent backward through time.

articles.title: Why preprint servers are increasing moderation — and what that means for researchers
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01203-6Repositories are adapting in response to AI-generated content and junk science, leading some people to question how much moderation is too much.
articles.content:
A level of peer review can happen for manuscripts submitted to preprint servers.Credit: Wasan Tita/iStock via Getty

The COVID‑19 pandemic had a major effect on scholarly communication, accelerating data sharing, peer review and the publication of preprints. Although preprints have been around for decades, the pandemic underscored the part they play in the rapid, free and open dissemination of scientific knowledge.

The proliferation of artificial-intelligence-generated manuscripts and junk science in the past few years has forced preprints to adapt once again. Some repositories seem to be tightening their quality-control and moderation procedures in an effort to protect against these threats.

Preprint moderators have to strike “a really, really difficult balance” between rapidly sharing new research and protecting the community from flawed or harmful material, says Natascha Chtena, who studies scholarly communication and open science at Simon Fraser University in Vancouver, Canada.

Chtena and Alice Fleerackers, a social scientist at the University of Amsterdam, have been looking into moderation practices at preprint servers. They’ve found that, under specific circumstances, moderators make judgements about the quality of submissions, often using criteria similar to those applied by academic journals.

Although these efforts are well intentioned, legitimate submissions can get caught in the crossfire, says Fleerackers.

Fleerackers, who has been researching the preprint ecosystem for six years, has experienced this herself. In 2025, she submitted a qualitative study on science journalism to a major repository, only to be told that it contained no new data or analysis. It was her second rejection by that repository that year – a stark change from previous years, when she’d had several submissions accepted by the repository in similar research areas.

Making it more difficult for genuine research to be published ins preprints “fundamentally goes against the spirit of open science and the commitment to advancing inclusive, rapid knowledge sharing that is at the heart of preprints’ value — for both science and society”, Fleerackers says.

This raises the question: how much moderation by a repository is too much?

No one-size-fits-all approach

Over the past three decades, dozens of preprint servers have been launched by academic communities, research institutes and publishers, with the goal of making new research publicly available before it has undergone peer review. Speed and accessibility are key qualities of these platforms, many of which emphasize in their policies that they avoid making judgements on the quality and significance of submissions.

It’s something that John Inglis, co-founder of preprint servers bioRxiv and medRxiv, takes seriously. “If there are signals that the provenance of a manuscript is not reliable, we investigate and may decline” it, he says. “But we do not judge scientific quality or value: that is for the community to assess once the manuscript is available.”

A hands-off approach to moderation is becoming increasingly difficult, however. During the pandemic, bioRxiv decided to automatically reject manuscripts that made predictions about treatments solely on the basis of computational work, for example, owing to concerns over misleading health information being shared.

In a preprint posted on the server MetaArXiv in 20241, Chtena, Fleerackers and their co-authors highlight the variety of measures that these repositories are now using to screen submissions. The study, which was based on interviews with 14 managers and moderators of these repositories, including Inglis, found that some sites use advisory boards to make decisions on difficult manuscripts, whereas others rely on the moderators’ “gut feeling” as to whether to accept or reject a submission.

Many interviewees said they used reputation markers or “trust cues”, such as formatting that resembles that of a scientific paper or details of an author’s affiliations and publication record, to judge whether a submission was legitimate.

A repository’s size, scope and capacity can often influence its moderation practices. The manager of RINarxiv, an Indonesian preprint server that receives fewer than 20 submissions each month, speaks directly to the submitting authors to learn about the intention and story behind their manuscript before accepting or rejecting it, Chtena and Fleerackers’ study found.

Nature Index 2025 Research Leaders

At LatArXiv, a preprint server for Latin American research, basic integrity checks, such as verifying the authors’ ORCID profiles and publication history, are performed by a small group of editors, founder and publisher Patricio Pantaleo told Nature Index.

Such methods would be impossible to implement at arXiv, which receives more than 25,000 submissions a month. Instead, the repository uses an ‘endorsement system’ to verify that a submission comes from members of the scientific community. The system requires new submitters to have both an institutional e-mail address and previous authorship of a paper in the same scientific domain. If they do not, they must get a personal endorsement from an established arXiv author.

These requirements were tightened to stem “the flood of low-quality, non-scientific submissions”, arXiv announced in a blogpost in January.

A changing landscape
articles.reasoning: The article discusses the evolving moderation practices of preprint servers in response to challenges like AI-generated content and junk science, highlighting efforts to maintain the integrity and rapid dissemination of scientific knowledge. This has broad significance for the global research community and society by protecting the quality of open scientific communication.
articles.personality_title: Preprint servers improve checks to keep science reliable and fast
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Preprint servers are online platforms where scientists share research papers before official review. The COVID-19 pandemic made these servers very important for sharing knowledge quickly. But recently, fake and AI-made papers have caused problems for these platforms.

**What happened** – To fight low-quality and misleading papers, preprint servers have started checking submissions more carefully. Researchers Natascha Chtena and Alice Fleerackers studied how these servers decide what to accept. Some servers use expert boards, others trust moderators’ judgments or check authors’ backgrounds. For example, arXiv requires new authors to be verified by other scientists to stop poor-quality papers.

**Impact** – These new rules help keep scientific papers trustworthy while still sharing them quickly. But sometimes real research gets rejected by mistake, which worries scientists like Fleerackers. Servers must balance sharing fast with protecting quality. Different servers use different methods depending on how many papers they receive and their size.

**What's next step** – Preprint servers will keep adjusting their checks to handle new challenges like AI-generated content. They will try to find better ways to spot low-quality papers without stopping good research. Studies like the one by Chtena and Fleerackers will help improve these systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Preprint servers are updating their review methods to protect scientific quality while keeping research open and fast for everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les serveurs de prépublications renforcent les contrôles pour garantir une science fiable et rapide
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les serveurs de prépublications sont des plateformes en ligne où les scientifiques partagent leurs travaux avant la revue officielle. La pandémie de COVID-19 a rendu ces serveurs très importants pour diffuser rapidement les connaissances. Mais récemment, des articles faux ou générés par intelligence artificielle ont posé problème.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour lutter contre les articles de mauvaise qualité ou trompeurs, les serveurs de prépublications ont commencé à vérifier les soumissions plus soigneusement. Les chercheurs Natascha Chtena et Alice Fleerackers ont étudié comment ces serveurs décident d’accepter un article. Certains utilisent des comités d’experts, d’autres font confiance aux jugements des modérateurs ou vérifient les profils des auteurs. Par exemple, arXiv demande aux nouveaux auteurs d’être validés par d’autres scientifiques pour éviter les articles de faible qualité.

**Impact** – Ces nouvelles règles aident à garder la confiance dans les articles scientifiques tout en les partageant rapidement. Mais parfois, de vraies recherches sont rejetées par erreur, ce qui inquiète les chercheurs comme Fleerackers. Les serveurs doivent trouver un équilibre entre rapidité et qualité. Les méthodes varient selon la taille et le nombre de soumissions des serveurs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les serveurs de prépublications vont continuer d’adapter leurs contrôles face aux nouveaux défis comme les contenus générés par IA. Ils chercheront de meilleures façons de repérer les articles faibles sans bloquer les bonnes recherches. Les études comme celle de Chtena et Fleerackers aideront à améliorer ces systèmes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les serveurs de prépublications mettent à jour leurs méthodes pour protéger la qualité scientifique tout en gardant la recherche ouverte et rapide pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: Los servidores de preprints mejoran controles para mantener la ciencia fiable y rápida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los servidores de preprints son plataformas en línea donde los científicos comparten sus investigaciones antes de la revisión oficial. La pandemia de COVID-19 hizo que estos servidores fueran muy importantes para compartir conocimiento rápido. Pero recientemente, artículos falsos o generados por inteligencia artificial han causado problemas.

**Qué pasó** – Para combatir trabajos de baja calidad o engañosos, los servidores de preprints comenzaron a revisar las publicaciones con más cuidado. Los investigadores Natascha Chtena y Alice Fleerackers estudiaron cómo deciden aceptar artículos. Algunos usan comités expertos, otros confían en el juicio de moderadores o revisan el perfil de los autores. Por ejemplo, arXiv exige que nuevos autores sean respaldados por otros científicos para evitar trabajos de baja calidad.

**Impacto** – Estas nuevas reglas ayudan a mantener la confianza en los artículos científicos y a compartirlos rápido. Pero a veces investigaciones reales son rechazadas por error, lo que preocupa a científicos como Fleerackers. Los servidores deben equilibrar rapidez y calidad. Las formas de hacerlo varían según el tamaño y cantidad de artículos recibidos.

**Próximo paso** – Los servidores de preprints seguirán ajustando sus controles para enfrentar nuevos retos como contenido generado por IA. Buscarán mejores maneras de detectar trabajos pobres sin detener buenas investigaciones. Estudios como el de Chtena y Fleerackers ayudarán a mejorar estos procesos.

**Frase clave** – Los servidores de preprints actualizan sus métodos para proteger la calidad científica mientras mantienen la investigación abierta y rápida para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an open book transforming into a network of interconnected glowing manuscripts floating gently above it, with delicate scales balancing symbols of rapid sharing and careful moderation, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and subtle blues.
